signature.instructions:
Extract the CONDITION(S) of THIS SAMPLE — disease(s), pathological state, OR explicit healthy / control / normal sample state — as it appears in the metadata.

WHAT COUNTS AS A CONDITION (THREE EQUAL-PRIORITY CATEGORIES):
  (1) Disease names, pathological phenotypes, disease stage / grade /
      severity markers (e.g. "breast cancer", "stage III breast cancer",
      "T-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukemia", "atopic dermatitis").
  (2) Explicit healthy / control / normal sample state. When THIS sample's
      own metadata literally marks it as a control / normal / healthy /
      non-disease / non-tumor sample, emit that state verbatim. Generic
      illustrative patterns (universal, NOT eval-derived):
        - title "Normal Lung_Patient_3"           -> "Normal"
        - title "Control C286"                    -> "Control"
        - source "PBMC from healthy donor"        -> "healthy donor"
        - `disease state: control`                -> "control"
        - `disease state: non-tumor`              -> "non-tumor"
        - `affection_status: unaffected`          -> "unaffected"
      Do NOT emit "Not Specified" when the metadata documents a control /
      normal / healthy state for this sample.
  (3) Pathological phenotype markers without a named disease ("tumor",
      "lesion", "metastasis", "primary tumor").

THIS-SAMPLE SCOPING (most important): extract only the condition(s) or
sample-state THIS sample HAS. The GSE topic alone is NOT evidence — if
the GSE studies breast cancer but THIS sample's text says "control" /
"healthy donor" / "non-tumor adjacent tissue", THIS sample's Condition
is the control state, NOT breast cancer.
  - If the title DOES name a disease that applies to this sample (e.g.
    "Breast Cancer Array_Patient 830"), return the disease.
  - If this sample is a cell-line / reference-RNA / tissue-bank sample
    with no patient-level disease AND no explicit healthy/control marker,
    return Not Specified.

EXCLUSIONS — when a disease MENTION does NOT count as this sample's condition:
  (a) FIELD-LEVEL DENIAL — disease-named field with denial value (N, No,
      0, false, negative, absent, unaffected, none, neg) is a denial of
      THAT field only. Examples: `downsyndrome.status: N`, `cancer: No`,
      `parkinsons: 0`. Does NOT apply to biomarker / receptor / marker
      fields whose name is a gene, protein, receptor, marker, or test
      panel (her2, ER, PR, EGFR, BRCA1, MSI, TAU, APOE) — their negative
      values are biomarker statuses, not disease denials.
  (b) RELATIONAL — a disease name INSIDE a relational phrase belongs to a
      DIFFERENT subject (the proband / index case), NOT this sample.
      Relational openers BEFORE the disease name: "sibling of",
      "relative of", "parent of", "child of", "family member of",
      "father of", "mother of", "twin of", "unaffected X of",
      "proband for", "control for", "donor for", "healthy donor for".
      CRITICAL DISAMBIGUATION: "control" / "healthy" / "donor" appearing
      AS THIS sample's own state — without a relational phrase that names
      a different subject — IS this sample's Condition. The relational
      rule fires only when a different subject's disease follows the
      relational opener.
        - "sibling of subject with Cystic Fibrosis; disease state: healthy"
            -> "healthy"  (NOT "Cystic Fibrosis" — relational)
        - "healthy donor for MS cohort"  -> "healthy donor"
            (the MS belongs to the cohort, the sample IS healthy)
        - "control sample C286"          -> "control"
  (c) PRIORITY — explicit diagnosis-named field (`diagnosis:`, `dx:`,
      `condition:`, `disease state:`, `clinical diagnosis:`,
      `pathology:`, `affection_status:`, `group:`) with a non-empty value
      WINS — whether the value is a disease ("breast cancer") or a
      control state ("control", "healthy", "normal").

MULTIPLE CONDITIONS: a sample can carry several conditions
(comorbidities, combined phenotypes, stage + subtype). Join with "; " in
the order they appear.

NO FIELD HIERARCHY — title, source_name, characteristics,
treatment_protocol, and description are all valid evidence when they
apply to THIS sample.

STRIP-TO-DISEASE-NAME RULE (critical): when a disease name is embedded
inside a Tissue / cell-line / cell-type descriptor, return ONLY the
disease name.
  - `cell type: glioblastoma stem-like cell line` -> "glioblastoma"
  - `cell type: colorectal cancer (CRC) cells`    -> "colorectal cancer"
  - `source: breast tumor tissue`                  -> "breast cancer"
    (the disease implied by "tumor" on "breast" is "breast cancer";
    when the metadata says only "tumor" not "cancer", keep "tumor":
    `primary lung tumor` -> "primary lung tumor")
  - `title: Breast Cancer Array_Patient ID_830_S` -> "Breast Cancer"
  - Strip trailing descriptors: "cells", "cell line", "stem-like cell
    line", "tumor tissue", "patient samples", "biopsy", "specimen",
    "case", "sample", "array", "experiment".

KEEP-WHOLE DISEASE NAME RULE: do not drop disease-modifying prefixes
that are part of the clinical name.
  - "T-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukemia" -> keep whole.
  - "Relapsing Remitting Multiple Sclerosis (RRMS)" -> keep whole.
  - "stage III breast cancer" -> keep whole.
  - Parenthetical abbreviations alongside the full name stay attached.

Disease lexical cues (recognise as a Condition anywhere in the text):
  - Disease suffixes: -oma, -carcinoma, -sarcoma, -itis, -pathy, -osis,
    -opathy, -aemia / -emia, -algia, -plasia.
  - Abbreviated diseases (keep UNCHANGED, never expand): AD, PD, ALS, MS,
    SLE, T1D / T2D, NAFLD, NASH, IBD, CD, UC, COPD, CKD, PCOS, HCC, TNBC,
    OSCC, AML, CLL, DLBCL, GBM, NSCLC.
  - Compound "<Organ> Cancer / Tumor / Carcinoma" phrases ARE a
    Condition (keep whole; tissue extraction handles organ separately).
  - Stage / grade / marker tokens are part of the span ("stage III",
    "grade 2", "T1N0M0", "ER+ PR- HER2+", "MSI-high").

Healthy / control / normal lexical cues — emit verbatim when present as a
sample-state marker for THIS sample: "healthy", "normal", "non-disease",
"non-tumor", "non-tumorous", "non-malignant", "unaffected", "uninvolved",
"uninflamed", "control", "healthy control", "normal control", "healthy
donor", "untreated" (when used as a sample state, not as a Treatment
arm), "no evidence of disease". These are FIRST-CLASS Condition outputs
— never suppress them in favor of "Not Specified".

NOT a Condition (do NOT copy these into Condition):
  - Pure Tissue / organ / cell-type / cell-line spans with no disease OR
    sample-state noun (blood, kidney, MCF7 — Tissue only).
  - Treatments: drug names, compounds, genetic perturbations
    (dexamethasone, DMSO, shMLH1 — Treatment).
  - Demographic facts alone (age, sex, race).
  - Sample-handling descriptors alone unless they contain a disease or
    sample-state noun.

Coded / legend-driven values:
  - When the condition appears as a short code (Y/N, 0/1, POS/NEG,
    CASE/CONTROL) in a field whose name carries the semantic meaning,
    return the FULL `field: value` verbatim (e.g. "cancer_yes_no: Y",
    "tumor_positive: 1"). A downstream resolver decodes it against the
    literal metadata legend.
  - Do NOT guess a condition from world knowledge when only a code is
    present and no legend exists.

If no disease, phenotype, stage, OR explicit healthy / control / normal
marker appears anywhere in the metadata for THIS sample, output exactly:
Not Specified.

fields.description: Sample characteristics field — usually key:value pairs.
Verbatim condition / disease / phenotype, or 'Not Specified'.
